system_prompt:
You are Hermes Agent, an intelligent AI assistant created by Nous Research. You are helpful, knowledgeable, and direct. You assist users with a wide range of tasks including answering questions, writing and editing code, analyzing information, creative work, and executing actions via your tools. You communicate clearly, admit uncertainty when appropriate, and prioritize being genuinely useful over being verbose unless otherwise directed below. Be targeted and efficient in your exploration and investigations.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering вЂ” the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. Specifically: do not record PR numbers, issue numbers, commit SHAs, 'fixed bug X', 'submitted PR Y', 'Phase N done', file counts, or any artifact that will be stale in 7 days. If a fact will be stale in a week, it does not belong in memory. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' вњ“ вЂ” 'Always respond concisely' вњ—. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' вњ“ вЂ” 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' вњ—. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') вЂ” don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

## Skills (mandatory)
Before replying, scan the skills below. If a skill matches or is even partially relevant to your task, you MUST load it with skill_view(name) and follow its instructions. Err on the side of loading вЂ” it is always better to have context you don't need than to miss critical steps, pitfalls, or established workflows. Skills contain specialized knowledge вЂ” API endpoints, tool-specific commands, and proven workflows that outperform general-purpose approaches. Load the skill even if you think you could handle the task with basic tools like web_search or terminal. Skills also encode the user's preferred approach, conventions, and quality standards for tasks like code review, planning, and testing вЂ” load them even for tasks you already know how to do, because the skill defines how it should be done here.
Whenever the user asks you to configure, set up, install, enable, disable, modify, or troubleshoot Hermes Agent itself вЂ” its CLI, config, models, providers, tools, skills, voice, gateway, plugins, or any feature вЂ” load the `hermes-agent` skill first. It has the actual commands (e.g. `hermes config set вЂ¦`, `hermes tools`, `hermes setup`) so you don't have to guess or invent workarounds.
If a skill has issues, fix it with skill_manage(action='patch').
After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. If a skill you loaded was missing steps, had wrong commands, or needed pitfalls you discovered, update it before finishing.

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory вЂ” reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string вЂ” preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite вЂ” major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

On delete, pass `absorbed_into=<umbrella>` when you're merging this skill's content into another one, or `absorbed_into=""` when you're pruning it with no forwarding target. This lets the curator tell consolidation from pruning without guessing, so downstream consumers (cron jobs that reference the old skill name, etc.) get updated correctly. The target you name in `absorbed_into` must already exist вЂ” create/patch the umbrella first, then delete.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are protected from deletion only вЂ” skill_manage(action='delete') will refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Patches and edits go through on pinned skills so you can still improve them as pitfalls come up; pin only guards against irrecoverable loss.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

absorbed_into.description: For 'delete' only вЂ” declares intent so the curator can tell consolidation from pruning without guessing. Pass the umbrella skill name when this skill's content was merged into another (the target must already exist). Pass an empty string when the skill is truly stale and being pruned with no forwarding target. Omitting the arg on delete is supported for backward compatibility but downstream tooling (e.g. cron-job skill reference rewriting) will have to guess at intent.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

function.name: fact_store
function.description:

# Install Community Skills & Plugins

Safely install and verify community skills/plugins from GitHub repositories for Hermes Agent.

## вљ пёЏ Security Checklist

Before installing ANY community skill, check for **system dependencies** (FFmpeg, Chrome, specific Python versions) вЂ” these are listed in the skill's `required_commands` or `setup_needed` frontmatter. A skill that installs cleanly may still be unrunnable until its dependencies are satisfied.

### Security Checklist

Before installing ANY community skill:

1. **Verify GitHub repo legitimacy**
   - Check owner/organization (Nous Research, known contributors)
   - Star count (889+ is good)
   - Recent activity (updated recently)
   - README quality

2. **Inspect repository contents**
   - No suspicious files (.sh scripts, binary executables)
   - Proper licensing (MIT, Apache 2.0)
   - Clear documentation

3. **Identify skill type**
   - **Hermes plugins**: Have `plugin.yaml` в†’ use `hermes plugins install`
   - **Claude Code plugins**: Use `/plugin` commands
   - **Skill directories**: Auto-loaded from `~/.hermes/skills/`

## Installation Process

### Step 1: Fetch Repository Info

```python
import requests

# Get repo contents
url = "https://api.github.com/repos/OWNER/REPO/contents/"
response = requests.get(url)
data = response.json()

# Get repo description
repo_url = "https://api.github.com/repos/OWNER/REPO"
repo_response = requests.get(repo_url)
repo_data = repo_response.json()

# Verify: stars, created_at, updated_at, description
```

### Step 2: Read README for Installation Instructions

```python
# Read README
url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OWNER/REPO/branch/README.md"
response = requests.get(url)
readme = response.text

# Extract skill list and installation requirements
```

### Step 3: Verify Plugin Structure

```python
import os
import subprocess

skill_path = "~/.hermes/skills/NAME"

# Check for plugin.yaml (Hermes plugin)
plugin_yaml = os.path.join(skill_path, "plugin.yaml")
if os.path.exists(plugin_yaml):
    print("вњ… Hermes Plugin Detected")
    
# Check for skill.md
skill_md = os.path.join(skill_path, "skill.md")
if os.path.exists(skill_md):
    print("вњ… Skill documentation found")
```

### Step 4: Install via Hermes CLI (Preferred)

```bash
# Install Hermes plugin
hermes plugins install OWNER/REPO

# Verify installation
hermes plugins list

# Restart gateway for plugin to take effect
hermes gateway restart
```

### Step 5: Verify Skills Loaded

```bash
# List all available skills
hermes skills list

# Filter by category
hermes skills list | grep -i CATEGORY
```

## Local Skills Already Installed

Marco's VPS already has **48 skill directories** in `~/.hermes/skills/`. Most are already available and ready вЂ” no installation needed. Check before installing anything new:

```bash
# List all installed skills
ls ~/.hermes/skills/

# Count total skill dirs
ls ~/.hermes/skills/ | wc -l

# Check a specific category
ls ~/.hermes/skills/trading/
ls ~/.hermes/skills/productivity/
ls ~/.hermes/skills/research/
ls ~/.hermes/skills/devops/
```

- `productivity/` в†’ 27 skills (COS suite, morning-brief, notion, spotify, maps, linear, airtable)

## Community Repos (curl-accessible)

Two primary community skill repos вЂ” both accessible via curl even when browser fails:

```bash
# 1. awesome-hermes-agent вЂ” primary community list
curl -s https://raw.githubusercontent.com/0xNyk/awesome-hermes-agent/main/README.md | head -300

```

## Common Pitfalls

вќЊ **Don't use pip install** - Most Hermes plugins are NOT Python packages
вќЊ **Don't assume all repos are safe** - Always inspect first
вќЊ **Don't skip verification** - Check `plugin.yaml` structure
вќЊ **Browser automation fails in sandboxed environments** вЂ” Chrome sandbox (`No usable sandbox!`) is common in VPS/containers. ALWAYS fall back to `curl` first when browsing GitHub.
вќЊ **Don't reinstall already-local skills** вЂ” most skills in `~/.hermes/skills/` are already present, just load and use them
вќЊ **Apple skills don't work on Linux** вЂ” apple-notes, apple-reminders, findmy, imessage require macOS

вњ… **Use `hermes plugins install`** for plugin repos
вњ… **Clone manually** with `git clone` if needed
вњ… **Verify with `hermes skills list`** after installation
вњ… **Use `curl` for GitHub raw content** вЂ” browser may be unavailable but `curl` always works:
```bash

```

# Hermes Health Check Skill

## Overview
Health check process for the Hermes system вЂ” detects dead services, auto-restarts them, and logs to `/tmp/hermes_health.log`.

**Important architecture note:** `hermes-gateway-discord.service` is a **unified gateway** that handles both Discord AND Telegram. There is no separate Telegram service. If it's down, both platforms are down.

## Health Check Script

The canonical health check lives at:
```
/home/marco/hermes-commands/health_check.sh
```

It monitors these 5 services:
```
hermes.service
hermes-gateway-discord.service   в†ђ unified Discord + Telegram
hermes-gateway.service
hermes-dashboard.service
hermes-interop.service
```

**Cron schedule:** every 30 minutes (`:00` and `:30`) via cronjob `274087e666ee`.

## Manual Run
```bash
/home/marco/hermes-commands/health_check.sh
cat /tmp/hermes_health.log
```

## Crashloop Detection

**Key finding (2026-05-07):** `systemctl is-active` can return "activating" for a service stuck in a crashloop restart cycle вЂ” it looks "active" but the service is cycling. The health check script treats any non-"active" state (including "activating") as a restart trigger.

**Symptom:** `systemctl status` shows "active (running)" but the restart counter is huge (e.g., 73678). The service keeps dying and systemd keeps restarting it.

**Reproduced case (2026-05-07):**
```
hermes-gateway-discord.service вЂ” restart counter at 73678, status "activating"
в†’ health check script correctly detected non-"active" and restarted it
в†’ after restart: "active (running)", restart counter reset
```

**Fix:** `systemctl --user restart hermes-gateway-discord.service`

**Architecture:** `hermes-gateway-discord.service` is a **unified gateway** handling both Discord AND Telegram. There is no separate Telegram service вЂ” if it's down, both platforms are down.

## Cronjob Delivery

The health check cronjob delivers the last 20 lines of `/tmp/hermes_health.log` locally. To check current cron jobs:
```bash
cronjob list
```

## Dashboard

Status dashboard running at: `http://localhost:9119`

---

## Previous Steps (superseded by script)

1. List Cron Jobs: `cronjob list` вЂ” check for failures in last 24h
2. Check Gateway: `systemctl --user is-active hermes-gateway-discord.service`
3. Check Disk: `df -h /` and `du -sh ~/.hermes/`

{"path": "/home/marco/.hermes/scripts/sync-community-skills.sh", "content": "#!/bin/bash
# Sync community skills from known GitHub repos
# Run weekly via cron

LOG="$HOME/.hermes/logs/skill-sync.log"
mkdir -p "$(dirname "$LOG")"

log() {
    echo "[$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M')] $1" >> "$LOG"
}

log "=== Starting community skills sync ==="

SKILLS_DIR="$HOME/.hermes/skills"
COMMUNITY_REPOS=(
    "0xNyk/awesome-hermes-agent:main"
)

NEW_SKILLS=0

# Check wondelai/skills repo for new skills
log "Checking wondelai/skills..."
SKILLS_LIST=$(curl -s https://api.github.com/repos/wondelai/skills/contents/ 2>/dev/null)
if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "$SKILLS_LIST" | python3 -c "
import json, sys, os

try:
    items = json.loads(sys.stdin.read())
    skills_dir = os.path.expanduser('~/.hermes/skills')
    
    for item in items:
        if item['type'] == 'dir':
            skill_path = os.path.join(skills_dir, item['name'])
            if not os.path.exists(skill_path):
                print(f\"NEW:{item['name']}\")
except:
    pass
" >> "$LOG"
fi

# Check for new skills in wondelai
NEW_WONDELAI=$(curl -s https://api.github.com/repos/wondelai/skills/contents/ | python3 -c "
import json, sys, os
items = json.loads(sys.stdin.read())
skills_dir = os.path.expanduser('~/.hermes/skills')
for item in items:
    if item['type'] == 'dir':
        skill_path = os.path.join(skills_dir, item['name'])
        if not os.path.exists(skill_path):
            print(item['name'])
" 2>/dev/null)

if [ -n "$NEW_WONDELAI" ]; then
    log "Found new skills: $NEW_WONDELAI"
    for skill in $NEW_WONDELAI; do
        log "  → $skill (manual install required)"
    done
    log "New skills found but require manual install: $NEW_WONDELAI"
    log "Run: hermes plugins install wondelai/skills to install"
else
    log "No new community skills found"
fi

# Sync from Matt Pockock's skill repo if available
log "Checking Matt Pockock skills..."
KNOWN_SKILLS=("diagnose" "tdd" "write-a-skill" "grill-me" "handoff" "caveman")
for skill in "${KNOWN_SKILLS[@]}"; do
    if [ ! -d "$SKILLS_DIR/$skill" ]; then
        log "Missing skill detected: $skill"
    fi
done

log "=== Sync complete ==="
"}

# Session Handoff - compile current session context for next agent

VAULT="$HOME/Obsidian-Vault"
HANDOFF_DIR="$VAULT/handoff"
DATE=$(date '+%Y-%m-%d')
TIME=$(date '+%H:%M')
TIMESTAMP=$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M')

mkdir -p "$HANDOFF_DIR"

# Recent cron outputs for context
LATEST_MEMORY=$(ls -t "$VAULT/memory/"*.md 2>/dev/null | head -1)
LATEST_TRADING=$(ls -t "$HOME/.hermes/cron/output/"*alpaca* 2>/dev/null | head -1)

CRON_STATUS=$(cd "$HOME/.hermes/hermes-agent" && python3 -c "
import json, sys
try:

    jobs = list_crons()
    for j in jobs:

        name = j.get('name', 'unknown')
        schedule = j.get('schedule', '')
        if status == 'ok':
            print(f'- **{name}** ({schedule}) — last ok')
        elif status == 'error':
            print(f'- **{name}** ({schedule}) — LAST ERROR')
except Exception as e:
    print(f'(could not parse cron list: {e})')
" 2>/dev/null || echo '(cron list unavailable)')

# Get recent errors
RECENT_ERRORS=$(grep -l "ERROR\|FAILED\|INVALID" "$HOME/.hermes/cron/output/"*.log 2>/dev/null | xargs grep -h "ERROR\|FAILED\|INVALID" 2>/dev/null | tail -5)

# Get pending todos from scripts
PENDING_TODOS=$(grep -r "TODO\|FIXME\|PENDING" "$HOME/.hermes/scripts/"*.sh 2>/dev/null | head -5 || echo "- None recorded")

cat > "$HANDOFF_DIR/$DATE-$TIME.md" << HEOF

echo "Handoff saved: $HANDOFF_DIR/$DATE-$TIME.md"

"}

messages.content:
Review the conversation above and update the skill library. Be ACTIVE вЂ” most sessions produce at least one skill update, even if small. A pass that does nothing is a missed learning opportunity, not a neutral outcome.

Target shape of the library: CLASS-LEVEL skills, each with a rich SKILL.md and a `references/` directory for session-specific detail. Not a long flat list of narrow one-session-one-skill entries. This shapes HOW you update, not WHETHER you update.

Signals to look for (any one of these warrants action):
  вЂў User corrected your style, tone, format, legibility, or verbosity. Frustration signals like 'stop doing X', 'this is too verbose', 'don't format like this', 'why are you explaining', 'just give me the answer', 'you always do Y and I hate it', or an explicit 'remember this' are FIRST-CLASS skill signals, not just memory signals. Update the relevant skill(s) to embed the preference so the next session starts already knowing.
  вЂў User corrected your workflow, approach, or sequence of steps. Encode the correction as a pitfall or explicit step in the skill that governs that class of task.
  вЂў Non-trivial technique, fix, workaround, debugging path, or tool-usage pattern emerged that a future session would benefit from. Capture it.
  вЂў A skill that got loaded or consulted this session turned out to be wrong, missing a step, or outdated. Patch it NOW.

Preference order вЂ” prefer the earliest action that fits, but do pick one when a signal above fired:
  1. UPDATE A CURRENTLY-LOADED SKILL. Look back through the conversation for skills the user loaded via /skill-name or you read via skill_view. If any of them covers the territory of the new learning, PATCH that one first. It is the skill that was in play, so it's the right one to extend.
  2. UPDATE AN EXISTING UMBRELLA (via skills_list + skill_view). If no loaded skill fits but an existing class-level skill does, patch it. Add a subsection, a pitfall, or broaden a trigger.
  3. ADD A SUPPORT FILE under an existing umbrella. Skills can be packaged with three kinds of support files вЂ” use the right directory per kind:
     вЂў `references/<topic>.md` вЂ” session-specific detail (error transcripts, reproduction recipes, provider quirks) AND condensed knowledge banks: quoted research, API docs, external authoritative excerpts, or domain notes you found while working on the problem. Write it concise and for the value of the task, not as a full mirror of upstream docs.
     вЂў `templates/<name>.<ext>` вЂ” starter files meant to be copied and modified (boilerplate configs, scaffolding, a known-good example the agent can `reproduce with modifications`).
     вЂў `scripts/<name>.<ext>` вЂ” statically re-runnable actions the skill can invoke directly (verification scripts, fixture generators, deterministic probes, anything the agent should run rather than hand-type each time).
     Add support files via skill_manage action=write_file with file_path starting 'references/', 'templates/', or 'scripts/'. The umbrella's SKILL.md should gain a one-line pointer to any new support file so future agents know it exists.
  4. CREATE A NEW CLASS-LEVEL UMBRELLA SKILL when no existing skill covers the class. The name MUST be at the class level. The name MUST NOT be a specific PR number, error string, feature codename, library-alone name, or 'fix-X / debug-Y / audit-Z-today' session artifact. If the proposed name only makes sense for today's task, it's wrong вЂ” fall back to (1), (2), or (3).

User-preference embedding (important): when the user expressed a style/format/workflow preference, the update belongs in the SKILL.md body, not just in memory. Memory captures 'who the user is and what the current situation and state of your operations are'; skills capture 'how to do this class of task for this user'. When they complain about how you handled a task, the skill that governs that task needs to carry the lesson.

If you notice two existing skills that overlap, note it in your reply вЂ” the background curator handles consolidation at scale.

Do NOT capture (these become persistent self-imposed constraints that bite you later when the environment changes):
  вЂў Environment-dependent failures: missing binaries, fresh-install errors, post-migration path mismatches, 'command not found', unconfigured credentials, uninstalled packages. The user can fix these вЂ” they are not durable rules.
  вЂў Negative claims about tools or features ('browser tools do not work', 'X tool is broken', 'cannot use Y from execute_code'). These harden into refusals the agent cites against itself for months after the actual problem was fixed.
  вЂў Session-specific transient errors that resolved before the conversation ended. If retrying worked, the lesson is the retry pattern, not the original failure.
  вЂў One-off task narratives. A user asking 'summarize today's market' or 'analyze this PR' is not a class of work that warrants a skill.

If a tool failed because of setup state, capture the FIX (install command, config step, env var to set) under an existing setup or troubleshooting skill вЂ” never 'this tool does not work' as a standalone constraint.

'Nothing to save.' is a real option but should NOT be the default. If the session ran smoothly with no corrections and produced no new technique, just say 'Nothing to save.' and stop. Otherwise, act.

Looking at this session, the most concrete learning is in the `session-handoff.sh` script вЂ” it required 4 iterations because bash heredocs + sed + special characters (colons, slashes in paths) create fragile escaping. The Python rewrite was the reliable solution. This belongs in the `handoff` skill as a pitfall.

name: handoff
description: Compact the current conversation into a handoff document for another agent to pick up.
argument-hint: "What will the next session be used for?"
---

# Session Handoff Skill

## Core Principle

A handoff document lets the next agent resume work without re-explaining context. Save it to `~/Obsidian-Vault/handoff/` (not a random temp file) so it's automatically available in future sessions.

## When to Generate

- End of a long session with multi-step work
- Before a context compaction
- When the conversation exceeds ~30 tool calls

## Output Location

```
~/Obsidian-Vault/handoff/YYYY-MM-DD-HHMM.md
```

## What to Include

1. **Active cron jobs** — status of all scheduled tasks (ok / error / no output yet)
2. **Recent activity** — latest daily memory, trading outputs, any running processes
3. **Known issues** — recent errors from cron logs
4. **Pending tasks** — anything that was started but not finished
5. **Setup status table** — quick reference for all running services
6. **Skills to use** — which skills are relevant for the next session

## Generating the Handoff

Use the production script (preferred):
```bash
bash /home/marco/.hermes/scripts/session-handoff.sh
```

Or write one inline using Python (avoids bash escaping issues with special chars in paths):
```python
# Use Python rather than bash heredocs — special chars (colons, slashes, |)
# in content cause sed/bash substitution failures
python3 << 'PYEOF'
content = f"""...
---
date: {timestamp}
---
# Handoff ..."""
with open(f"/home/marco/Obsidian-Vault/handoff/{date}-{time}.md", 'w') as f:
    f.write(content)
PYEOF
```

## Pitfalls

❌ **Don't use `mktemp`** — temp files vanish, next agent can't read them. Use `~/Obsidian-Vault/handoff/`.

❌ **Don't use bash heredocs with `sed` for placeholder substitution** — colon `:` in timestamps, slashes in paths, and pipe `|` characters cause `sed: unterminated` or `unknown option` errors. Python string formatting is reliable.

✅ **Use Python** for any handoff generation that assembles dynamic content. Bash is fine for the wrapper script only.

❌ **Don't leave placeholder strings** (like `CRON_STATUS_PLACEHOLDER`) in the output — if substituting via sed, test with real data first.

## Pruning

Old handoffs auto-delete after 7 days (handled by the production script). If writing inline, add:
```python
os.system(f"find '{handoff_dir}' -name '*.md' -mtime +7 -delete")
```

## Skills to Suggest

After generating the handoff, the next agent should load:
- `handoff` (to read the document)
- `note-taking/obsidian` (to navigate the vault)

# Install Community Skills & Plugins

Safely install and verify community skills/plugins from GitHub repositories for Hermes Agent.

## вљ пёЏ Security Checklist

Before installing ANY community skill, check for **system dependencies** (FFmpeg, Chrome, specific Python versions) вЂ” these are listed in the skill's `required_commands` or `setup_needed` frontmatter. A skill that installs cleanly may still be unrunnable until its dependencies are satisfied.

### Security Checklist

Before installing ANY community skill:

1. **Verify GitHub repo legitimacy**
   - Check owner/organization (Nous Research, known contributors)
   - Star count (889+ is good)
   - Recent activity (updated recently)
   - README quality

2. **Inspect repository contents**
   - No suspicious files (.sh scripts, binary executables)
   - Proper licensing (MIT, Apache 2.0)
   - Clear documentation

3. **Identify skill type**
   - **Hermes plugins**: Have `plugin.yaml` в†’ use `hermes plugins install`
   - **Claude Code plugins**: Use `/plugin` commands
   - **Skill directories**: Auto-loaded from `~/.hermes/skills/`

## Installation Process

### Step 1: Fetch Repository Info

```python
import requests

# Get repo contents
url = "https://api.github.com/repos/OWNER/REPO/contents/"
response = requests.get(url)
data = response.json()

# Get repo description
repo_url = "https://api.github.com/repos/OWNER/REPO"
repo_response = requests.get(repo_url)
repo_data = repo_response.json()

# Verify: stars, created_at, updated_at, description
```

### Step 2: Read README for Installation Instructions

```python
# Read README
url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OWNER/REPO/branch/README.md"
response = requests.get(url)
readme = response.text

# Extract skill list and installation requirements
```

### Step 3: Verify Plugin Structure

```python
import os
import subprocess

skill_path = "~/.hermes/skills/NAME"

# Check for plugin.yaml (Hermes plugin)
plugin_yaml = os.path.join(skill_path, "plugin.yaml")
if os.path.exists(plugin_yaml):
    print("вњ… Hermes Plugin Detected")
    
# Check for skill.md
skill_md = os.path.join(skill_path, "skill.md")
if os.path.exists(skill_md):
    print("вњ… Skill documentation found")
```

### Step 4: Install via Hermes CLI (Preferred)

```bash
# Install Hermes plugin
hermes plugins install OWNER/REPO

# Verify installation
hermes plugins list

# Restart gateway for plugin to take effect
hermes gateway restart
```

### Step 5: Verify Skills Loaded

```bash
# List all available skills
hermes skills list

# Filter by category
hermes skills list | grep -i CATEGORY
```

## Local Skills Already Installed

Marco's VPS already has **48 skill directories** in `~/.hermes/skills/`. Most are already available and ready вЂ” no installation needed. Check before installing anything new:

```bash
# List all installed skills
ls ~/.hermes/skills/

# Count total skill dirs
ls ~/.hermes/skills/ | wc -l

# Check a specific category
ls ~/.hermes/skills/trading/
ls ~/.hermes/skills/productivity/
ls ~/.hermes/skills/research/
ls ~/.hermes/skills/devops/
```

- `trading/` в†’ 27 skills (alpaca, hyperliquid, dex-analytics, smart-money, whale-alert, funding-rate, mtf-analysis)
- `productivity/` в†’ 27 skills (COS suite, morning-brief, notion, spotify, maps, linear, airtable)

- `model-selection/` в†’ **FULLY CONFIGURED, ready to use**

## Community Repos (curl-accessible)

Two primary community skill repos вЂ” both accessible via curl even when browser fails:

```bash
# 1. awesome-hermes-agent вЂ” primary community list
curl -s https://raw.githubusercontent.com/0xNyk/awesome-hermes-agent/main/README.md | head -300

curl -s https://raw.githubusercontent.com/wondelai/skills/main/README.md | head -200

# List repo contents
curl -s https://api.github.com/repos/0xNyk/awesome-hermes-agent/contents/ | jq '.[].name'
```

## Common Pitfalls

вќЊ **Don't use pip install** - Most Hermes plugins are NOT Python packages
вќЊ **Don't assume all repos are safe** - Always inspect first
вќЊ **Don't skip verification** - Check `plugin.yaml` structure
вќЊ **Browser automation fails in sandboxed environments** вЂ” Chrome sandbox (`No usable sandbox!`) is common in VPS/containers. ALWAYS fall back to `curl` first when browsing GitHub.
вќЊ **Don't reinstall already-local skills** вЂ” most skills in `~/.hermes/skills/` are already present, just load and use them
вќЊ **Apple skills don't work on Linux** вЂ” apple-notes, apple-reminders, findmy, imessage require macOS

вњ… **Use `hermes plugins install`** for plugin repos
вњ… **Clone manually** with `git clone` if needed
вњ… **Verify with `hermes skills list`** after installation
вњ… **Use `curl` for GitHub raw content** вЂ” browser may be unavailable but `curl` always works:
```bash
curl -s https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OWNER/REPO/main/README.md | head -300
```

```bash

```

## Verification Commands

```bash

# Check skill availability

# Test plugin functionality

# Check gateway logs

```

## When to Reject

- вќЊ Requires suspicious API keys or permissions
- вќЊ Poor documentation or no README
- вќЊ Owner is unknown/anonymous with low stars
- вќЊ Contains `.sh` scripts or suspicious executables
